en.extract:
The Cuban green woodpecker (Xiphidiopicus percussus) is a species of woodpecker in the family Picidae and tribe Melanerpini, known locally in Cuban Spanish as carpintero verde (literally "green woodpecker"). It is the only species within the genus Xiphidiopicus and is one of two woodpeckers endemic to Cuba. It is the most widespread and common woodpecker in Cuba, inhabiting primarily woodlands, as well as dry and wet forests, pine forests and mangroves. The population of the Cuban green woodpecker is stable and its status is listed as "Least Concern".


Appearance
A medium-sized and colorful bird, the Cuban green woodpecker is similar to a sapsucker in general shape and size. Featuring olive green overparts and yellow, streaked underparts, the Cuban green woodpecker usually appears crested, and features a bright red upper breast, as well as a black chin contrasting a white face and supercilium. In males, a red crown is present, while females possess black crowns with white stripes. The area around the cloaca, or the crissum, is yellow with black barring.
Its beak is short and straight with a progressively darkening coloration, from dark blue to black from base to tip dark blue to black coloration. They have been shown to have brown irises and grayish green tarsi and toes 
Cuban green woodpeckers show slight sexually dimorphic qualities; females have shorter bills and are generally significantly smaller than their male counterparts. Juveniles will show more barring and streaking on their breasts and lack the depth of color adults have in their plumage.
They typically measure between 21 and 25 cm (8.3 and 9.8 in) in length and weigh approximately 48 to 97 g (1.7 to 3.4 oz).


Taxonomy

The Cuban green woodpecker as a species is unique in both genetics and appearance. Historically, it was presumed that the Cuban green woodpecker (Xiphidiopicus percussus), and the Hispaniolan woodpecker (Melanerpes striatus) were sister taxa and there was speculation that it had relationships to the genera Sphyrapicus and Melanerpes. According to research done in 2006, "X. percussus is the sister taxon to the Melanerpes woodpeckers, which appear to group into a single distinct clade." It was also found that Xiphidiopicus percussus is not the sister taxon to M. striatus  and that the genus Sphyrapicus diverged earlier than Xiphidiopicus, which diverged during the late Miocene-early Pliocene, and originated in Central America or North America.
In terms of appearance, no other Cuban species of woodpecker unmarked green upperparts. The Yellow-bellied Sapsucker (Sphyrapicus varius) is similar in coloration and also has a facial pattern that is reminiscent to that of Cuban green woodpecker; however, the sapsucker's upperparts are white and black and has a broad black band across its breast.


Subspecies
The species can be divided into the following subspecies, with only two being formerly recognized:

X. p. insulaepinorum - found in the Cantiles Keys, Isla de la Juventud and Archipiélago Jardines de la Reina (Cayo Caballones).
X. p. percussus - found on the archipelagos of Sabana and Camagüey
X. p. cocoensis
X. p. gloriae
X. p. marthae
X. p. monticola


Habitat and distribution
The Cuban green woodpecker is only found in Cuba, but is extremely common on the island. Its natural habitats are wet and dry forests, mangroves, open woodland with palms, and pine forests, lowland moist forests, and heavily degraded former forest.


Behavior
The Cuban green woodpecker is found most frequently in pairs, and less frequently in small groups. It is able to remain completely still for long periods and will forage for dead insects on dead limbs at any height within its habitat. They are very territorial, especially during breeding season, and use active vocalization, utilizing both drumming and vocal signals, and active pursuit, pursuing threats while flicking their wings, in order deter intruders in their nests, which is usually the West Indian woodpecker (Melanerpes superciliaris).
Similarly to all other woodpeckers, its flight is undulating.


Vocalizations


Calls and songs
The Cuban green woodpecker's call is a short, harsh "jhhhorr, jhhhorr, jhhhorr," as well as a higher pitched "yeh-yeh-yeh."  The call is usually single noted with double notes occasionally being uttered.
It may sometimes produce a slurred, two-note call "ta-ha", which is similar to the Yellow-bellied Sapsucker (Sphyrapicus varius)'s call. It will use its call mostly while protecting nest and chicks from intruder.


Diet
The Cuban green woodpecker's is primarily an insectivore, focusing on large insects, but will also consume fruits and small frogs occasionally. It has also been documented taking eggs of other birds such as herons nesting in mangroves. It will also feed on flower nectar, especially Cordia sebestena. While perching itself on the plant or on a nearby branch to reach the flowers, it will insert its bill into the floral tube to drink the nectar for a few seconds, by doing so its bill will come into contact with anthers and stigma inside the floral tube, making the bird then act as a pollinator.
Males and females utilize different ways to exploit food sources, as the male is larger than the female.


Reproduction


Courtship
There is very little known about the courtship of the Cuban green woodpecker. Usually, woodpeckers perform flutter-aerial-display including a gliding flight with wings held well above the back, accompanied by calls. Courtship feeding by male to female most likely occurs as well. They are probably monogamous.


Breeding season, nesting and hatching
Cuban green woodpeckers will nest in the cavities of tree, alive or dead, often 4–5 metres above the ground. The male will usually do most of the excavation for the nest-hole, and will endure the nesting duties with a relatively high contribution. The breeding season takes place between February and August, with peak in May–June, probably associated with rains. The female will lay 3–4 white eggs and both adults share the incubation, which is presumed to be between 9 and 14 days, however more information is currently lacking, and feed the chicks, with the females feeding the nestlings at a higher rate. The chicks are hatched completely naked and stay in the nest for about 3–4 weeks. They are sexually mature in the first year of life.


References


External links

BirdLife species factsheet for Xiphidiopicus percussus
"Xiphidiopicus percussus". Avibase. 
"Cuban green woodpecker media". Internet Bird Collection.
Cuban green woodpecker photo gallery at VIREO (Drexel University)
Cuban green woodpecker species account at Neotropical Birds (Cornell Lab of Ornithology)
Interactive range map of Xiphidiopicus percussus at IUCN Red List
Audio recordings of Cuban green woodpecker on Xeno-canto.

The '''Cuban green woodpecker''' ('''''Xiphidiopicus percussus''''') is a species of [[woodpecker]] in the family [[Picidae]] and tribe Melanerpini, known locally in [[Cuban Spanish]] as '''carpintero verde''' (literally "green woodpecker").<ref>{{Cite web|title=Birds of Cuba – An Internet Field Guide to Rare Cuban Birds|url=https://cubaexplorer.com/rare-and-beautiful-birds-of-cuba/|access-date=2021-11-16|website=cubaexplorer.com|language=en-US}}</ref> It is the [[Monotypic taxon|only species]] within the genus '''''Xiphidiopicus'''''<ref>{{Cite journal|last1=Shakya|first1=Subir B.|last2=Fuchs|first2=Jérôme|last3=Pons|first3=Jean-Marc|last4=Sheldon|first4=Frederick H.|date=November 2017|title=Tapping the woodpecker tree for evolutionary insight|journal=Molecular Phylogenetics and Evolution|language=en|volume=116|pages=182–191|doi=10.1016/j.ympev.2017.09.005|pmid=28890006|doi-access=free}}</ref> and is one of two woodpeckers [[endemic]] to [[Cuba]]. It is the most widespread and common woodpecker in Cuba, inhabiting primarily woodlands, as well as dry and wet forests, pine forests and [[mangrove]]s.<ref name=":02">{{Cite web|title=Cuban Green Woodpecker|url=http://www.oiseaux-birds.com/card-cuban-green-woodpecker.html|access-date=2021-11-09|website=www.oiseaux-birds.com}}</ref><ref name=":1">{{Cite journal|last=Farnsworth|first=Andrew|date=2020-03-04|title=Cuban Green Woodpecker (Xiphidiopicus percussus)|url=https://birdsoftheworld.org/bow/species/cugwoo1/cur/introduction|journal=Birds of the World|language=en}}</ref><ref name=":2"/> The population of the Cuban green woodpecker is stable and its status is listed as "Least Concern".<ref name=":1" />

== Appearance ==
[[File:Cuban Green Woodpecker. Xiphidiopicus percussus - Flickr - gailhampshire (3).jpg|right|thumb|A male ''X.p percussus'' gripping a tree|342x342px]]A medium-sized and colorful bird, the Cuban green woodpecker is similar to a [[sapsucker]] in general shape and size.<ref name=":1" /><ref name=":2">{{Cite web|title=Xiphidiopicus percussus (Cuban Green Woodpecker) - Avibase|url=https://avibase.bsc-eoc.org/species.jsp?avibaseid=3DC3684D58C5FED3|access-date=2021-11-16|website=avibase.bsc-eoc.org}}</ref> Featuring olive green overparts and yellow, streaked underparts, the Cuban green woodpecker usually appears crested, and features a bright red upper breast, as well as a black chin contrasting a white face and [[supercilium]]. In males, a red [[Crown (anatomy)|crown]] is present, while females possess black crowns with white stripes.<ref name=":02" /><ref name=":1" /><ref name=":2" /><ref name=":3">{{Cite book |title=Handbook of the birds of the world |date=1992–2013|publisher=Lynx Edicions|others=Josep del Hoyo, Andrew Elliott, Jordi Sargatal, José. Cabot|isbn=978-84-87334-10-8|location=Barcelona|oclc=861071869}}</ref> The area around the [[cloaca]], or the crissum, is yellow with black barring.<ref name=":1" /><ref>{{Cite journal|last=Voss|first=Margaret A.|date=June 2012|title=Ornithological Literature|journal=The Wilson Journal of Ornithology|volume=124|issue=2|pages=420–427|doi=10.1676/1559-4491-124.2.420|s2cid=198153671|issn=1559-4491}}</ref>
Its beak is short and straight with a progressively darkening coloration, from dark blue to black from base to tip dark blue to black coloration.<ref name=":02" /><ref>{{Cite book|title=Birds of the West Indies|date=1998|publisher=C. Helm|others=Herbert A. Raffaele, Tracy Pedersen, Kristin Williams|isbn=0-7136-4905-4|location=London|oclc=44579236}}</ref><ref name=":3" /><ref name=":4">{{Cite journal|last=Koenig|first=Walter D.|date=July 1996|title=Woodpeckers: An Identification Guide to the Woodpeckers of the World Hans Winkler David A. Christie David Nurney|journal=The Auk|volume=113|issue=3|pages=726–727|doi=10.2307/4089007|jstor=4089007|issn=0004-8038}}</ref> They have been shown to have brown irises and grayish green tarsi and toes <ref name=":4" />

Cuban green woodpeckers show slight sexually dimorphic qualities; females have shorter bills and are generally significantly smaller than their male counterparts. Juveniles will show more barring and streaking on their breasts and lack the depth of color adults have in their [[plumage]].

They typically measure between {{convert|21|and|25|cm|in|abbr=on}} in length and weigh approximately {{convert|48|to|97|g|oz|abbr=on}}.<ref name=":02" /><ref name=":1" /><ref name=":4" />

== Taxonomy ==
[[File:Cuban Green Woodpecker. Xiphidiopicus percussus - Flickr - gailhampshire (2).jpg|right|thumb|Underparts of a male ''Xiphidiopicus percussus''|345x345px]]
The Cuban green woodpecker as a species is unique in both genetics and appearance.<ref name=":5">{{Cite journal|last1=Overton|first1=Lowell C.|last2=Rhoads|first2=Douglas D.|date=November 2006|title=Molecular phylogenetic relationships of Xiphidiopicus percussus, Melanerpes, and Sphyrapicus (Aves: Picidae) based on cytochrome b sequence|journal=Molecular Phylogenetics and Evolution|volume=41|issue=2|pages=288–294|doi=10.1016/j.ympev.2006.05.014|pmid=16814571|issn=1055-7903}}</ref> Historically, it was presumed that the Cuban green woodpecker (''Xiphidiopicus percussus)'', and the [[Hispaniolan woodpecker]] (''Melanerpes striatus'') were sister taxa and there was speculation that it had relationships to the genera ''[[Sapsucker|Sphyrapicus]]'' and ''[[Melanerpes]]''. According to research done in 2006, "''X. percussus'' is the sister taxon to the ''Melanerpes'' woodpeckers, which appear to group into a single distinct clade."<ref name=":5" /> It was also found that ''Xiphidiopicus percussus'' is not the sister taxon to ''M. striatus''  and that the genus ''Sphyrapicus'' diverged earlier than ''Xiphidiopicus,'' which diverged during the late [[Miocene]]-early [[Pliocene]], and originated in [[Central America]] or [[North America]].<ref name=":5" />

In terms of appearance, no other Cuban species of woodpecker unmarked green upperparts. The [[Yellow-bellied sapsucker|Yellow-bellied Sapsucker]] (''Sphyrapicus varius'') is similar in coloration and also has a facial pattern that is reminiscent to that of Cuban green woodpecker; however, the sapsucker's upperparts are white and black and has a broad black band across its breast.

=== Subspecies ===
The species can be divided into the following subspecies, with only two being formerly recognized:<ref name=":1" />

* '''''X. p. insulaepinorum''' -'' found in the Cantiles Keys, Isla de la Juventud and Archipiélago Jardines de la Reina (Cayo Caballones).<ref name=":6">{{Cite journal|date=July 1905|title=Bangs and Zappey's 'Birds of the Isle of Pines' Birds of the Isle of Pines Outram Bangs|journal=The Auk|volume=22|issue=3|page=329|doi=10.2307/4070184|jstor=4070184|issn=0004-8038}}</ref>
* '''''X. p. percussus''''' - found on the archipelagos of Sabana and Camagüey<ref name=":7">{{Cite journal|last=Rodríguez-Cabrera|first=Tomás M.|date=2017-08-01|title=Predation by a Cuban Racer, Cubophis cantherigerus pepei (Squamata: Dipsadidae) on an endemic Cuban Green Woodpecker, Xiphidiopicus percussus percussus (Piciformes: Picidae)|journal=Reptiles & Amphibians|volume=24|issue=2|pages=120–123|doi=10.17161/randa.v24i2.14164|issn=2332-4961|doi-access=free}}</ref>
* ''X. p. cocoensis''
* ''X. p. gloriae''
* ''X. p. marthae''
* ''X. p. monticola''

== Habitat and distribution ==
The Cuban green woodpecker is only found in Cuba, but is extremely common on the island. Its natural [[habitat]]s are wet and [[Cuban dry forests|dry forests]], mangroves, open woodland with palms, and pine forests, [[Cuban moist forests#Lowland forests|lowland moist forests]], and heavily degraded former forest.<ref name=":02" /><ref name=":1" /><ref>{{Cite book|last1=Nuttall Ornithological Club.|url=https://www.biodiversitylibrary.org/item/257421|title=Memoirs of the Nuttall Ornithological Club|last2=Club|first2=Nuttall Ornithological|date=1943|publisher=The Club|location=Cambridge, Mass.}}</ref>

== Behavior ==

The Cuban green woodpecker is found most frequently in pairs, and less frequently in small groups.<ref name=":02" /><ref name=":1" /> It is able to remain completely still for long periods and will forage for dead insects on dead limbs at any height within its habitat.<ref name=":1" /> They are very territorial, especially during breeding season, and use active vocalization, utilizing both drumming and vocal signals, and active pursuit, pursuing threats while flicking their wings, in order deter intruders in their nests, which is usually the [[West Indian woodpecker]] (''Melanerpes superciliaris'').<ref name=":02" /><ref name=":1" />

Similarly to all other woodpeckers, its flight is [[Undulatory locomotion|undulating]].<ref name=":02" />
[[File:Cuban Green Woodpeckers. Xiphidiopicus percussus - Flickr - gailhampshire.jpg|thumb|385x385px|Pair of ''X. percussus'' perching on branches]]
=== Vocalizations ===

==== Calls and songs ====
The Cuban green woodpecker's call is a short, harsh "''jhhhorr, jhhhorr, jhhhorr,''" as well as a higher pitched "''yeh-yeh-yeh''."<ref name=":02" /><ref name=":1" />  The call is usually single noted with double notes occasionally being uttered.

It may sometimes produce a slurred, two-note call "ta-ha", which is similar to the [[Yellow-bellied sapsucker|Yellow-bellied Sapsucker]] (Sphyrapicus varius)'s call. It will use its call mostly while protecting nest and chicks from intruder.<ref name=":1" /><ref name=":3" /><ref name=":7" />

==== Diet ====
The Cuban green woodpecker's is primarily an [[insectivore]], focusing on large insects, but will also consume [[fruit]]s and small [[frog]]s occasionally.<ref name=":1" /><ref name=":02" /><ref name=":3" /><ref name=":6" /> It has also been documented taking eggs of other birds such as [[heron]]s nesting in mangroves.<ref name=":02" /><ref name=":1" /> It will also feed on flower [[nectar]], especially ''[[Cordia sebestena]]''.<ref name=":8">Dalsgaard, B. (2011). Nectar-feeding and pollination by the Cuban Green Woodpecker (Xiphidiopicus percussus) in the West Indies. ''Ornitología Neotropical'', ''22'', 447–451.</ref><ref name=":1" /> While perching itself on the plant or on a nearby branch to reach the flowers, it will insert its bill into the [[Hypanthium|floral tube]] to drink the nectar for a few seconds, by doing so its bill will come into contact with [[Stamen|anthers]] and [[Stigma (botany)|stigma]] inside the floral tube, making the bird then act as a [[pollinator]].<ref name=":8" />
Males and females utilize different ways to exploit food sources, as the male is larger than the female.<ref name=":02" />
[[File:Cordia sebestena L. หมันแดง Family name BORAGINACEAE (5).jpg|right|thumb|297x297px|''[[Cordia sebestena]]'']]
=== Reproduction ===

==== Courtship ====
There is very little known about the courtship of the Cuban green woodpecker. Usually, woodpeckers perform flutter-aerial-display including a [[gliding flight]] with wings held well above the back, accompanied by calls.<ref name=":02" /><ref>{{Cite journal|last=KELLAM|first=JAMES S.|title=Pair Bond Maintenance in Pileated Woodpeckers at Roost Sites During Autumn|date=June 2003|journal=The Wilson Bulletin|volume=115|issue=2|pages=186–192|doi=10.1676/02-098|s2cid=86215324|issn=0043-5643}}</ref> [[Courtship display|Courtship]] feeding by male to female most likely occurs as well.<ref name=":02" /> They are probably monogamous.<ref name=":02" />

==== Breeding season, nesting and hatching ====
Cuban green woodpeckers will nest in the cavities of tree, alive or dead, often 4–5 metres above the ground.<ref name=":1" /><ref name=":02" /> The male will usually do most of the excavation for the nest-hole, and will endure the nesting duties with a relatively high contribution.<ref name=":02" /> The breeding season takes place between February and August, with peak in May–June, probably associated with rains.<ref name=":92">Kirkconnell, A. (2000). Variación morfológica del Carpintero Verde Xiphidiopicus percussus en Cuba. Cotinga. 14: 94–98.http://www.neotropicalbirdclub.org/wp-content/uploads/2016/05/Cotinga-14-2000-94-98.pdf</ref><ref name=":1" /><ref name=":02" /> The female will lay 3–4 white eggs and both adults share the incubation, which is presumed to be between 9 and 14 days, however more information is currently lacking, and feed the chicks, with the females feeding the nestlings at a higher rate.<ref name=":02" /><ref name=":92" /><ref name=":1" /> The chicks are hatched completely naked and stay in the nest for about 3–4 weeks.<ref name=":92" /> They are sexually mature in the first year of life.<ref name=":92" />
[[File:Cuban Green Woodpecker (Xiphidiopicus percussus) (12793061613).jpg|thumb|289x289px|A male excavating a nest from an arboreal termite nest]]
